content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A site redesign can look like the goal is the moment you publish the new design. But the real crux sits behind it: how you carry over the “address” (URL) of the old pages. The look got cleaner, yet the search traffic and bookmarks you’d built up snap the instant you go live.
This article is written for directors at production agencies who take on client site redesigns. The design and build went off without a hitch, and then the post-launch traffic gives you a scare — here’s that stumble we see so often on redesign projects, and the handover routine we built with MONJI+, holding nothing back. If you have a redesign coming up, we hope even one part helps with the handover.

The moment we went live, search traffic snapped
First, the scariest thing in a redesign is the gap in an invisible “change of address.”
When a redesign changes the structure of a page’s address (URL), people and search engines arriving at the old URL lose their way. Without setting up 301 redirects that forward the old URL to the new one automatically, the old pages turn into 404 errors.
A common pattern in a redesign is to redirect only the prominent main pages by hand, without building a mapping of old to new URLs. The top page and main services get forwarded. But the settings for the finer, lower-level pages drop out entirely. “We launched it” and “we carried it over” are completely different things.

“Just the main pages,” we thought — but the gaps were hiding in the lower levels
This gap usually surfaces after launch.
A few days after going live, a message comes in from the client: “My old bookmark doesn’t work anymore.” Look at Google Analytics, and organic search traffic has clearly dropped from the week after launch. The people who’d been landing on lower-level pages were hitting 404s across the board — that’s the sequence.
The tricky part is that the list of which old URL should forward to which new URL isn’t on hand. The old site is right about to be taken down, and just rebuilding the mapping afterward takes days. Without a record, you can neither notice the gaps nor fix them.

We kept the old-to-new URL mapping as a “register” in the Wiki
So what we started from the next redesign onward was keeping the URL handover as a register.
In the project Wiki, we write out “old URL / new URL / redirect needed? / owner,” line by line. We started building this from the design stage of the redesign, not at the last minute before launch. Where each page should go is visible at a glance here.
We register the pre-launch checks in MONJI+’s checklist feature. We can call it up right from the screen where we create the Feedback, so we can line up items like this every time:

• Did you decide, for every old URL, whether a redirect is needed?

• Are the forwarding targets for the main paths (top, contact, main services) correct?

• Did you update and submit sitemap.xml with the new URL structure?

• Did you remove the noindex and staging settings at launch?

• Are any redirects chained in multiple hops (A→B→C)?
Setting gaps also get caught by the website issue detection. Just enter a URL and it crawls the whole site, detects 404s and broken links, and you can route them straight into Feedback. It can cover desktop/mobile display differences and Basic-auth staging environments too, so we can now find the “holes still open” before launch.

After launch, we confirmed in the numbers whether traffic came back
With the gaps closed, we make a point of not ending things at launch.
With the Google Analytics integration, we check within the project whether organic search traffic has come back. For pages still down, we prioritize adding redirects there. We turn each fix into a single Feedback with an owner, due date, and priority, so who moves and when doesn’t float off. Feedback with near due dates lines up on the My Page dashboard, so what’s left after launch is clear at a glance every morning.
For sharing with the client too, carving out only the in-progress Feedback via a public link means no re-explaining “how far along are we now” out loud.

Even so, “which pages to keep” is ours to decide
Even when the system prevents the misses, there’s a part only people can hold.
Which old pages are worth carrying over, which to merge, which to retire — this design judgment isn’t something a tool makes for you. The ones who know best the intent a site was built with, and what its traffic has been, are us, who’ve run it. The register and the checklist are the foundation for making that call quickly and correctly; they don’t make the call for us.
So we draw the line: leave finding the gaps to the system, and keep the final “keep this page,” “fold it in here” decision in our own hands. Carrying over redirects is unglamorous work, but it’s the job of not dropping the traffic and trust you’ve built up in the single day of a launch.

MONJI+, the WebOps platform born from the field
MONJI+, the WebOps platform, was born to solve the challenges we’ve felt firsthand in the field.
MONJI+ brings the “people” involved in website operation together into one “team,” and works across every phase of website operation to solve their challenges.
From the start, we’ve never aimed to build a “finished product.”
The challenges truly worth facing are out in the field of website operation, and that’s exactly why we’ve kept engaging with the real voices there.
Taking in each voice one by one, we’ve layered on updates that resolve small points of friction, and new features have grown out of the field’s challenges. Through that accumulation, MONJI+ has become something supported by users across 77 countries.
▼ Learn more about MONJI+ here
https://monji.tech/plus/
A world where the people working in website operation can say, with pride, “I love this work.”
▼ To make that real, we’re waiting to hear your voice
https://monji.tech/plus/co-creation/

Summary

• When a redesign changes the URL structure, missing 301 redirects turn old pages into 404s, snapping search traffic and bookmarks the moment you go live.

• Redirecting only the main pages by hand tends to leave lower-level gaps; without a mapping you can neither notice nor fix them.

• We keep the old-to-new URL mapping as a register in the Wiki, and call up pre-launch checks as a checklist from the Feedback screen.

• Remaining gaps get caught as 404s and broken links by the website issue detection and filed as Feedback, and we confirm the traffic recovery in numbers via the Google Analytics integration.

• Which pages to keep, merge, or retire isn’t left to a tool; it’s held by us, who know the site’s intent.
